pages.text: The hill town slept under a silver bowl of sky. Lamps twinkled along the winding streets, and the night smelled of damp earth and cardamom. <name> was staying with Dada at the old railway station, where rusted tracks met mossy platforms and pigeons slept on lamp posts.

Dada had given her his brass telescope with a carved sun on its rim. 'The sky tells stories,' he said. Every night they sat on the platform listening to frogs, distant dogs, and faint tabla beats from nearby houses.

One night, near midnight, a strange whistle rose — soft and long, like a sigh made of stars. No trains ran at that hour. When <name> looked up, a glowing train hovered above the rails, its carriages made of light. A sign shimmered: STAR EXPRESS.

A conductor wearing a comet-feathered hat bowed. 'All aboard,' he said, his voice like wind through bamboo. <name> climbed in, and the train drifted into the night sky, weaving among constellations that hummed like music.

The first carriage sang ragas. Stars leaned close, humming softly. The next held tiny lanterns — children’s dreams of planting trees, painting murals, and learning music. In one lantern she saw her future self teaching kids to read under the banyan tree.
Another compartment held forgotten wishes — paper boats, folded stars, and notes glowing faintly. A gray-haired woman smiled, holding a paper sail from her long-ago promise to visit the sea.

Suddenly, the conductor frowned. 'A star is missing. Without it, one village will wake colder and dimmer.' He turned to <name>. 'Your grandfather’s telescope holds the key.' She remembered Dada’s note: 'Look deeper. Listen truer.'

Through the telescope, <name> saw moonlight twist into a rhythm — Dha-Dhin-Dhin-Dha. 'Ragas and rhythms,' she whispered. Humming softly, she played the tala with her heart. The constellations stirred, and a trembling star answered back.

The Star Express slowed near a gap in the sky. A lost star drifted, dim and shivering. <name> sang Dada’s melody, gentle and bright. The star’s light steadied, then flared — shooting home with a joyful chime. The sky filled with music and gold.

When the train returned, Dada waited with two steaming cups of chai. The telescope gleamed. Later that night, <name> found a new note inside its case: 'Shared wishes burn brightest. Take one star’s light and pass it on.'

At sunrise, she gathered her friends and painted a mural of stars in the village square. Each child made a wish — but for someone else. And from that day on, when the constellations shimmered unusually bright, people would smile and say, '<name> must have ridden the Star Express again.'
